text: Carr borrowed a powerful metaphor from Marshall McLuhan: the medium is the message. Every communication technology, McLuhan argued, reshapes human perception independently of the content it carries. The clock did not merely tell time — it taught people to think of the day as a sequence of discrete, measurable units. The map did not merely show geography — it trained the eye to see landscape as abstract, bird's-eye space. By this logic, the internet does something far more consequential than deliver information: it trains the mind to process the world in the internet's own image — fragmented, hyperlinked, ceaselessly interrupted, optimized for speed over depth.
question: What example does the passage use to illustrate McLuhan's claim that the clock reshaped human perception?

options.text: It made people more punctual and socially coordinated
It encouraged people to value efficiency over creativity
It taught people to think of the day as discrete, measurable units
It replaced natural rhythms with artificial scheduling
